analogy: Think of "Human Resources" through the chapter's own opening images: Social and Political Life 74 Chapter 6 Confronting Marginalisation In the previous chapter, we read about two different groups and their experiences of inequality and discrimination. Though powerless, such groups have fought, protested and struggled against being excluded or dominated by others. They have attempted... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Human Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that social and Political Life 74 Chapter 6 Confronting Marginalisation In the previous chapter, we read about two different groups and their experiences of inequality and discrimination.

The text explains that though powerless, such groups have fought, protested and struggled against being excluded or dominated by others.

The text explains that they have attempted to overcome their situation by adopting a range of strategies in their long history.

The text explains that religious solace, armed struggle, self improvement and education, economic uplift – there appears to be no one way of doing things.

The text explains that in all cases, the choice of struggle has depended on the circumstances that the marginalised find themselves in.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will read about some of the ways in which groups and individuals challenge existing inequalities.

The text explains that adivasis, Dalits, Muslims, women and other marginal groups argue that simply by being citizens of a democratic country, they possess equal rights that must be respected.

The text explains that many among them look up to the Constitution to address their concerns.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will see why the Constitution of India is something that marginalised groups invoke in the course of their struggles.

The text explains that as part of this, we will look at how rights are translated into laws to protect groups from continued exploitation and we will also look at the government’s efforts to formulate policies to promote the access of these groups to development.

The text explains that 75 Confronting Marginalisation The Constitution, as you have learnt in the first chapter of this book, lays down the principles that make our society and polity democratic.

The text explains that they are defined in and through the list of Fundamental Rights that are an important part of the Constitution.

The text explains that these rights are available to all Indians equally.

The text explains that as far as the marginalised are concerned, they have drawn on these rights in two ways: first, by insisting on their Fundamental Rights, they have forced the government to recognise the injustice done to them.

The text explains that second, they have insisted that the government enforce these laws.

The text explains that in some instances, the struggles of the marginalised have influenced the government to frame new laws, in keeping with the spirit of the Fundamental Rights.

The text explains that his direct, courageous challenge inspired many and even today Kabir’s poetry is sung and appreciated by Dalits, marginalised groups and those critical of social hierarchies in U.P., Rajasthan, Punjab, Madhya Pradesh, Bengal, Bihar and Gujarat.

The text explains that only she Who is free from delusion In this poem, Kabir is challenging the priest by directly questioning at him about where untouchability came from.

The text explains that likewise, throughout the 1970s and 1980s Adivasi people successfully organised themselves and demanded equal rights and for their land and resources to be returned to them.

The text explains that firstly, it lists modes of humiliation that are both physically horrific and morally reprehensible and seeks to punish those who (i) force a member of a Scheduled Caste or a Scheduled Tribe to drink or eat any inedible or obnoxious substance; … (iii) forcibly removes clothes from the person of a member of a Scheduled Caste or a Scheduled Tribe or parades him or her naked or with painted face or body or commits any similar act which is derogatory to human dignity… Secondly, it lists actions that dispossess Dalits and Adivasis of their meagre resources or which force them into performing slave labour.

The text explains that it directed every department/ministry of the union government and state governments to verify the facts within six months.

The text explains that the introduction to the final Act states that this Act is meant to undo the historical injustices meted out to forest dwelling populations in not recognising their rights to land and resources.

The text explains that policy: A stated course of action that provides direction for the future, sets goals to be achieved or lays out principles or guidelines to be followed and acted upon.

The text explains that re-read the story on Rathnam as well as the provisions of the 1989 Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act.

The text explains that now list one reason why you think he used this law to file a complaint.

The text explains that janu, believe that Adivasis can also use this 1989 Act to fight against dispossession?

The text explains that is there anything specific in the provisions of the Act that allows her to believe this?

The text explains that the poems and the song in this Unit allow you to see the range of ways in which individuals and communities express their opinions, their anger and their sorrow.

The text explains that in class, do the following two exercises: (a) Bring to class a poem that discusses a social issue.

The text explains that work in small groups with two or more poems to discuss their meaning as well as what the poet is trying to communicate.

The text explains that (b)Identify a marginalised community in your locality.

The text explains that write a poem, or song, or draw a poster etc to express your feelings as a member of this community.

The text explains that gLOSSARY Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation 2024-25

Source: NCERT Class 8 Resources and Development, Chapter 6: Human Resources

prelims_traps: Do not treat 77 Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation and GLOSSARY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Human Resources; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Human Resources through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 77 Chapter 6: Confronting Marginalisation and GLOSSARY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
